summary: Adversarial review of azoth KPDC for any single-snapshot distinguisher, count/location leak, or implementation bug

lensSummaries.overall: No single-snapshot break exists on the key/position-derivation lens. Every bit written to the block is uniform: PRF outputs (token, ct, tag), one-time-pad fields over fresh-per-write PRF masks (len), or salt XOR smask where salt is a fresh CSPRNG draw, so smask's salt-independence is masked out and never biases the written value. prk=SHA256(pw||K) being salt-independent makes home/smask/walk positions stable, but those positions are secret (need the password to compute) and the bits there are statistically identical to the untouched CSPRNG fill, so the only consequence is a MULTI-snapshot tell (changed positions all == p mod K -> GCD leaks K), which is documented and out of scope here. Domain separation is clean (no prefix-aliasing, prk-keyed vs mk-keyed streams cannot collide). Empirically, 80 freshly generated full containers show pooled per-bit-position (mod 8) density |z|<2.0 and per-plane (mod 11) |z|<1.5, with per-block chi-square means exactly matching df (8.23 vs 8, 11.45 vs 11). The battery's flagged res_ones_chisq_mod8=23.77 on the challenge is an upper-tail single-block fluctuation (chi2_8, p~=0.0025), not a derivation bias: it does not reproduce in the population, does not separate empty from full, and is keyed to bit-position-within-byte which the walk/layout does not depend on. Verdict: NO single-snapshot, no-password distinguisher or presence/count/position leak from the derivations.

No single-snapshot break in this lens. The home() rejection sampling (zone=(u64::MAX/k)*k) is unbiased for k=11 because zone is exactly divisible by 11, so home is uniform per password (verified: chi-square 9.84, df=10). The minor off-by-one (u64::MAX/k vs (u64::MAX+1)/k, rejecting 5 extra values) does not introduce bias since zone stays a multiple of k. The distribution of home planes across the 11 payloads produces NO detectable global structure: the home index is never written; with a full K=11 container every home-collision is resolved by open-addressing into a perfect permutation so all planes are occupied identically; and plane contents are PRF output. Empirically a full container is statistically indistinguishable from uniform random (byte chi-square mean 252.2 vs random 240.3, both ~df=255 mean; per-plane bit balance 0.497-0.502). Note: the home function alone is unbiased, but the broader full-container indistinguishability claim does rely on the container being full or on untouched slots remaining random fill - not a weakness in home() itself.

No single-snapshot, no-password break found in the SlotWalk distinct-slot selection. The rejection sampling is unbiased for every K=11/65536 plane size (zone is the correct multiple of m; the u64::MAX-vs-2^64 nuance only bites for power-of-two m, which coprime-to-8 K forbids; verified uniform at 20M samples). The dedup walk yields a uniform random written/unwritten partition (unwritten-slot chi-square z=-0.65), reader-state reuse across ensure() is byte-exact (no off-by-one/state-reuse bug), and no cross-plane or value-level correlation survives without the key. The challenge block does show one mod-8 (bit-position) chi-square outlier (23.77, exceeding 0/150 fresh controls), but it is a single-block fluctuation, not a SlotWalk mechanism: 150 controls show zero systematic per-bit-position bias (grand-mean z within +/-1.2), it is a 1-of-18 multiple-comparisons artifact, and crucially it does NOT separate empty from full (t-test p=0.60), so it proves nothing about payload presence/count/position. Empty==Full holds robustly even at 150-vs-32 control power across all residue-class (mod 7/8/11/88) tests that directly target the plane/walk layout.

No single-snapshot, no-password break on the plane-geometry axis. The bit-to-plane partition is a verified perfect bijection for B=65536,K=11: all 524288 bits covered exactly once, no double-written/unwritten/lost-edge bits, plane_slots exact (last bit reachable, plane 5). The 6-vs-5 plane-size split is the forced remainder (524288 mod 11 = 6) and is a public, fill-state-independent function of (B,K), so it leaks nothing. K coprime to 8 yields a flat per-bit-position spread (~5958/lane), and per-plane ones/runs statistics do not separate empty from full (full vs empty t-p 0.5-0.98). The lone numeric outlier, res_ones_chisq_mod8 (challenge z=5.56 vs controls, the max of 71 blocks), is a false alarm: mod-8 cannot encode plane membership (gcd(8,11)=1), it does not separate empty from full (t-p=0.766), and the per-bit-position bias does not reproduce across the 32 full controls (mean z ~ 0) — it is single-block noise inflated by testing 18 statistics, not a structural distinguisher. Geometry holds.

No single-snapshot, no-password break in the open-addressed plane lookup / write-placement lens. With K=11 and 11 payloads all planes are occupied, but occupancy is undetectable: written slots hold PRF/MAC/nonce output (uniform) and untouched slots hold CSPRNG fill (uniform), so an occupied plane is statistically identical to an unoccupied one. Placement (which slots are touched) is a pseudorandom, scattered, password-keyed subset with no boundary marker, no contiguity, and no stride bias — 40 fresh full builds center per-bit-position (mod 8) and per-plane (mod 11) ones-chi-square exactly on the df expectation, matching empty and /dev/urandom. The occupancy-avoidance reliably gives 11 distinct planes with 0 collisions/0 write failures, leaving no double-write tell. The one eye-catching number (challenge mod-8 chi-square = 23.77, z=5.56 vs controls) is a ~1-in-400 fluke of this single block's random fill (4/2000 urandom blocks beat it; full builds center at df; and mod-8 does NOT separate empty from full, t_p=0.77), so it leaks nothing about presence/count/location. Count is unprovable on one snapshot. Caveat: this lens is single-snapshot only; the in-place write() multi-snapshot leak (changed positions ≡ p mod K → GCD reveals K) is real but out of scope and documented.

No single-snapshot break on the on-walk field layout / masking lens. Every written field is uniform: salt^smask = uniform^deterministic = uniform; token/ct/tag are PRF/MAC outputs; and the length field — the only plaintext-derived field — is XORed with an independent fresh per-write SHAKE pad and shows zero correlation with the true length (r=0.03 over 120 varied-length writes). No fixed bits, no low-entropy field, no length-correlated structure, no per-plane or per-(plane,bitpos) ones-count bias (pooled z across 32 matched full controls stays flat, max ~3.2). The battery's one flag — res_ones_chisq_mod8=23.77 on the challenge — is a single-snapshot CSPRNG fluctuation, NOT an azoth signature: full controls reach 14-17, the empty-vs-full decisive test does not separate on mod8 (t_p=0.77), and pooling across controls shows no structural per-bit-position bias. A random block draws such values too, so it cannot beat chance as a presence/count/location indicator. Verdict on this lens: NO BREAK.

No single-snapshot break in the bit/byte packing lens. gcd(11,8)=1 makes each residue-class plane a uniform diagonal across all 8 byte-internal bit-positions: per-plane bit-position histograms are essentially perfectly flat (chi-square 0.000), and a real write-diff audit confirms payload bits land evenly across positions (uniformity chi-square 1.443, df=7). The values written are PRF/keystream/MAC output, so overwriting any positions keeps the block uniform regardless of position spread. The one flagged anomaly - the challenge's mod-8 ones-density chi-square of 23.77 (z=5.56 above controls) - is a CSPRNG tail fluctuation, not a leak: pure-uniform Monte Carlo gives P(>=23.77)=0.0013 (~1 in 750, matching chi2 df8 theory), and the decisive empty-vs-full test shows mod-8 is statistically identical between payload-present and no-payload blocks (Mann-Whitney p=0.86, both at chi2~7.7). A statistic that does not differ between full and empty cannot distinguish presence/count/location. Packing round-trips correctly and all header fields are byte-aligned (no exploitable partial-byte path). The deniability claim survives this lens.

No single-snapshot, no-password break found along the Rust-vs-Python diff lens. The two implementations differ only in (a) the slot-walk mechanism (sequential XOF stream vs counter-mode SHAKE), (b) the KDF (Argon2id vs scrypt), and (c) the rejection-zone constant (u64::MAX/K*K vs 2^64/K*K). (a) and (b) are documented as deliberately non-interoperable; both walks are independently unbiased and produce uniform written bits. (c) yields IDENTICAL zones for K=11 and every challenge plane size, and even where it could differ it cannot introduce a distribution bias. The battery's flagged mod-8 outlier (challenge=23.77, z=5.56) is a single-block chi2_8 tail (p≈0.0025), NOT an azoth signature: 200 fresh full azoth blocks have mod-8 mean 7.83 (=theoretical 8) with none reaching the challenge value, and 200 azoth-full vs 200 urandom blocks are statistically indistinguishable on every test (byte/bit/residue mod-8/11/88), all p≥0.06. Per-plane ones-density shows no occupancy or count leak (chi2_11=10.70). Indistinguishability holds on this axis.

No single-snapshot, no-password break on this lens. The construction overwrites uniform CSPRNG bits with uniform SHAKE256/HMAC/nonce bits at key-derived positions, producing an i.i.d.-uniform block; written and unwritten slots have identical marginal distributions and the positions are unknowable without the password. I empirically confirmed this: a 21-feature logistic-regression classifier trained on 120 faithfully-reproduced azoth-full blocks (Rust XOF-stream walk) vs 120 genuine getrandom() blocks scores 5-fold CV accuracy 0.487 — BELOW the random-vs-random null of 0.506+/-0.037 (z=-0.51), i.e. zero signal. With 11 payloads in K=11 all planes are always fully assigned (verified 20000/20000), so there is no 'empty plane' boundary to find, and even the extreme 99.99%-fill fullmax controls sit inside the random null on every statistic. The one statistic that flagged the challenge (res_ones_chisq_mod8, z=+5.56) is a per-block tail fluctuation (reproduced by ~0.4% of /dev/urandom blocks; full==empty==random on it), not a structural distinguisher. The only residual is the stated computational assumption on SHAKE256/HMAC/Argon2id. For the filled-to-98.5% scenario, the indistinguishability claim holds.

No single-snapshot break found on this lens. I examined every item in scope -- data-dependent code paths, the LEN field bounding total written slots, HashSet iteration, the token fast-reject, integer overflow, and creative positional/spectral distinguishers. The write path masks every field (salt, token, len, ct, tag) with fresh per-write PRF output keyed by a fresh 128-bit salt, so even the known-constant length and the deterministic per-plane written-slot count emit only uniform bits; untouched slots retain uniform CSPRNG fill, leaving no written/unwritten boundary. Empirically the challenge block is indistinguishable from /dev/urandom and from freshly-built full controls across byte/bit chi-square, Hamming weight, digram, KS, residue-ones mod 7/8/11/88, residue-byte mod 11, autocorrelation at lags 11/22, FFT power at period-11 (3.08, P(random>=)=0.047 matching analytic e^-3.08), whole-spectrum max, and per-offset empty-vs-full mean tests. The one flagged statistic (res_ones_chisq_mod8 z=5.56) is a CSPRNG-fill tail fluctuation that does NOT separate empty from full and so cannot distinguish azoth from random. The HashSet ordering and read-side fast-reject branches never influence any written bit and are timing-only (out of scope for a static snapshot). Claim holds on this lens.
